header.stance: Higher real yields drove an orderly Treasury selloff as energy inflation risk receded
header.title: Daily FI Market Intelligence

long_thesis.english_markdown:
## Opening Fixed-Income Thesis

The June 22 close extended the post-FOMC real-rate reset even as improving US-Iran diplomacy reduced the energy tail. Treasuries sold off across the curve, with the two-year yield at 4.24% and the 10-year at 4.51%. The maturity pattern was less dramatic than the prior week’s front-end shock, yet its composition was more revealing: the 10-year real yield reached 2.28% as 10-year breakeven inflation eased to 2.23%. Investors therefore demanded more real compensation despite cheaper oil, preserving the Fed’s restrictive signal while assigning less weight to a persistent energy-driven inflation premium.

That configuration continued the central interpretation from the previous open session, with an important modification. The June 18 bull flattening had treated Chair Kevin Warsh’s inflation commitment as protection for the long end while leaving policy-sensitive maturities exposed. After the Juneteenth closure, the curve kept the flattening accumulated since the Fed meeting, but duration no longer rallied. Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee’s concern about persistent services inflation and a New York Fed research model’s higher inflation and real-rate estimates made the rise in real yields more than a mechanical holiday catch-up.

Three transmission channels shaped the close. First, reduced forward guidance and a more data-responsive Fed kept the expected policy path firm, raising real discount rates across maturities. Second, progress toward a durable Middle East settlement pushed crude lower and compressed inflation compensation, limiting the long-end term-premium response without delivering a nominal bond rally. Third, a crowded corporate calendar, led by prospective technology and AI-related borrowing, showed that financing remained available but forced duration markets to contemplate much more supply as equity investors reassessed the same capital-intensive spending cycle.

The counterforce was the absence of a broad funding break. Bill auctions drew substantial bids, high-grade issuers continued to market large transactions, and high-yield fund losses were modest beside the Treasury adjustment. Risk assets were mixed rather than uniformly defensive, with semiconductor shares advancing even as a severe SpaceX decline weighed on the broader market. The close is best understood as a higher-real-rate session inside an orderly credit system: diplomacy reduced one inflation risk, but it did not yet dislodge the Fed’s tighter reaction function or the global competition for capital.

## UST Rates, Fed Pricing, Real Yields, And Curve Structure

Treasury yields rose in a nearly parallel daily move. The two-year added five basis points to 4.24%, the five-year rose six to 4.29%, and the 10- and 30-year each increased five basis points, to 4.51% and 4.95%. The five-year’s slight underperformance placed the greatest pressure around the belly, where policy expectations, upcoming coupon supply and corporate-rate exposure intersect. It was a selloff in duration, but not a renewed steepening driven by distant fiscal or inflation risk.

The curve retained the flattening built since the Fed meeting. The 2s10s spread closed unchanged on the day at 27 basis points and was 13 basis points flatter over one week. The 2s30s spread also held steady at 71 basis points, leaving it 19 basis points flatter over the week. The 2s5s10s butterfly rose two basis points to minus 17 as the five-year lagged, while the 2s10s30s butterfly was unchanged at minus 17. A stable daily slope alongside higher outright yields showed that the session reinforced the prevailing policy regime without creating a new long-end premium.

Inflation-linked securities supplied the clearest explanation. The five-year real yield rose five basis points to 2.01%, the seven-year gained six to 2.13%, and the 10-year increased seven to 2.28%. Twenty-year real yields rose six basis points to 2.58%, while the 30-year added five to 2.75%. At 10 years, the real-yield increase exceeded the nominal move because breakeven inflation fell two basis points to 2.23%. The market lowered its inflation-compensation requirement while raising the real hurdle rate, a combination aligned with cheaper energy and a less permissive Fed.

Goolsbee reinforced the distinction between a temporary energy shock and persistent underlying pressure. He described inflation as above target and moving in the wrong direction, with the rise in services prices particularly troubling because it was not readily explained by tariffs or the Iran-related energy surge. His willingness to reduce forward guidance also matched Warsh’s institutional direction. Less reliance on pre-committed paths can improve policy credibility, but it leaves the front end more sensitive to each activity and inflation release.

The New York Fed’s June DSGE update added a research-based real-rate argument. The model raised its 2026 core PCE forecast to 3.1% from 2.5% in March, lifted 2026 GDP growth to 1.2% from 1.0%, and increased its estimate of the real natural rate for the fourth quarter to 2.0% from 1.9%. The projection is a staff research-model output rather than the institution’s policy forecast. Even so, the simultaneous upward revisions to inflation, growth and the equilibrium real rate described the combination that made nominal yields rise despite falling oil and breakevens.

The long end still benefited from a credibility channel relative to the front end. The Fed’s shorter communication and willingness to react more aggressively to data can reduce the chance that inflation persistence becomes embedded in distant rates. That helps explain why 2s10s remained far flatter than before the FOMC even as the entire curve cheapened on Monday. The competing risk is greater front-end volatility: fewer signals about the next decision increase uncertainty about the timing of adjustments, and that uncertainty can spill into the belly when macro data or supply arrives.

Bills separated immediate cash conditions from the expected policy path. Four- and six-week coupon-equivalent yields fell three and one basis points to 3.63% and 3.65%, while the eight-week yield rose one basis point to 3.73%. From 13 weeks onward, yields moved higher: the 13-week closed at 3.79%, the 17-week at 3.85%, the 26-week at 3.97% and the 52-week at 4.03%. The upward slope from the shortest bills toward one year reflected higher prospective policy rates rather than a common rise in overnight cash pressure.

Treasury’s $89 billion 91-day auction stopped at a 3.695% high discount rate, equivalent to a 3.782% investment rate, with a 2.68 bid-to-cover ratio. Indirect bidders accepted $52.087 billion of the $87.168 billion competitive award, compared with $4.238 billion for directs and $30.843 billion for primary dealers. The security is issued and settles June 25. The reported cover and distribution showed that a large post-holiday bill offering cleared with broad participation even as the market marked the policy path higher.

The $77 billion 182-day auction extended the cash-demand reading farther along the bill curve. It stopped at a 3.840% high discount rate and a 3.970% investment rate, with 2.51 bid-to-cover and 97.87% allotted at the high. Indirect bidders took $40.073 billion, direct bidders $3.000 billion and dealers $32.481 billion of the $75.553 billion competitive amount. Its June 25 settlement coincides with the 91-day issue, making the combined cash transfer a separate balance-sheet event from Monday’s bidding.

The full rates picture was restrictive but orderly. Real yields rose across the curve, the belly marginally underperformed, and Fed communication kept near-term policy uncertainty elevated. Lower breakevens and stable curve slopes constrained the interpretation: investors were not extrapolating the energy shock into a larger long-run inflation premium. Bill demand also showed functioning cash absorption. The unresolved issue is whether incoming inflation validates the rise in real yields or whether diplomacy and weaker price pressure eventually restore the duration rally seen before the holiday.

## Credit, Funding, And Primary-Market Absorption

Credit total returns softened without signaling a broad withdrawal of financing. LQD fell 0.2659%, compared with declines of 0.0875% for HYG and 0.0934% for JNK. The larger investment-grade loss was consistent with its greater duration exposure during a Treasury selloff, while the smaller high-yield declines matched the mixed equity session. Primary markets remained active enough to distinguish higher benchmark yields from impaired market access.

June’s investment-grade calendar had already exceeded $120 billion by the middle of the month, a three-standard-deviation increase in first-half sales. The 15 largest transactions accounted for about 57% of issuance, including Nvidia’s roughly $25 billion deal, yet risk premiums had moved little through the supply wave. Concentration matters: deep demand for a handful of large technology borrowers can support aggregate absorption while leaving investors increasingly exposed to the same AI capital-spending theme.

SpaceX became the largest prospective test of that demand. Banks began investor calls for a debut investment-grade offering with maturities ranging from five to 30 years and a targeted size of at least $20 billion. Proceeds are intended to refinance a bridge loan of roughly the same amount, which represents most of the company’s $29.1 billion of long-term debt. Moody’s, Fitch and S&P assigned Baa1, BBB+ and BBB ratings, respectively, while the company reported almost $101 billion of cash and equivalents as of June 19.

The ratings and liquidity support near-term access, but the financing model extends well beyond a bridge takeout. One analyst projection put SpaceX net debt above $400 billion by 2031 as AI infrastructure consumes cash, and technology companies have raised more than $300 billion of AI-linked debt across credit markets since November. Nvidia’s sale drew orders exceeding three times its size, demonstrating current capacity. SpaceX’s governance concentration, environmental exposure and expected cash burn define the conditions under which that capacity could become more discriminating.

Sony added a cross-border high-grade case. The Japanese company began calls for five- and 10-year dollar notes for general corporate purposes, its first US-dollar market return since a $1.5 billion sale in 1998. The expected A2 and A+ ratings place it well above the lower end of investment grade. Denso’s $500 million dollar note, Mitsubishi’s $1 billion sale and Toyota’s €1 billion transaction showed that Japanese issuers are diversifying funding as domestic policy tightening raises the relative appeal of offshore markets.

Convertible issuance offered a different route around higher cash borrowing costs. Robinhood marketed $2 billion of zero-coupon notes due in 2029 with a 60%-65% conversion premium. About $300 million was earmarked for share repurchases, and a derivative overlay was designed to offset dilution up to a targeted 125% premium to the reference share price. Pricing was scheduled after the New York close, so the session established terms and intended use rather than completed absorption.

India’s securitization market showed funding depth outside the headline dollar calendar. Asset-backed issuance reached 1.53 trillion rupees in the year ended March, with foreign banks’ share rising to about 35% from 28%-30% in the prior two fiscal years. Retail-loan pools can satisfy priority-sector requirements and consume less regulatory capital than some direct loans, giving overseas lenders a structural reason to participate beyond yield. The growth broadens funding for domestic credit even as global real rates rise.

Market infrastructure also improved the visibility of secondary liquidity. A UK consolidated bond tape went live covering more than 95% of bonds traded across venues, including UK activity in foreign sovereign and corporate debt. Greater transaction transparency can sharpen price discovery as the primary calendar expands and benchmark rates become more volatile. It does not remove credit differentiation, but it may reduce the informational advantage once held by participants able to combine multiple private feeds.

The credit conclusion was open access under increasing concentration. Large high-grade borrowers, equity-linked structures and securitized pools could still draw capital, and June supply had not forced a broad repricing of risk premiums. At the same time, SpaceX’s planned borrowing and the wider AI debt cycle raised the amount of duration the market may need to absorb. Higher real yields therefore remained a cost and valuation constraint even without evidence of a systemic funding interruption.

## Global Duration, Central Banks, And Fiscal Transmission

Developed-market rates delivered a divided signal on energy pass-through. The ECB gained room to avoid an immediate escalation, Canada reported a broader inflation acceleration, Japan maintained its normalization bias, and China left lending benchmarks unchanged. This divergence limited a synchronized global duration selloff, yet it also kept the foreign policy-rate floor high enough to compete with Treasuries and influence cross-border funding choices.

ECB President Christine Lagarde said the Middle East shock did not yet require a more forceful response because longer-term inflation expectations remained broadly anchored. Traders pared year-end tightening to 33 basis points from 37 after she spoke, and Germany’s two-year yield fell six basis points to 2.59%. The relief was conditional. Euro-area core inflation had accelerated to 2.6% from 2.2%, and markets still expected at least another quarter-point increase in the deposit rate to 2.5%.

Jose Luis Escrivá emphasized the remaining wage channel after energy costs had already passed into transport-related services. Oil below $80 and progress toward a final US-Iran agreement reduced the immediate shock, but the ECB still had to distinguish short-term expectation slippage from a durable second-round process. The Commission’s June confidence reading improved to minus 17.7 in the euro area and minus 17.0 in the EU, though both remained weak. Better sentiment softened the growth downside without resolving the inflation constraint.

Canada moved in a more restrictive direction. May CPI accelerated to 3.2% year over year from 2.8% and rose 1.0% on the month, or 0.5% after seasonal adjustment. Gasoline inflation reached 33.2%, but the measure excluding gasoline also firmed to 2.2% from 2.0%. Food prices rose 3.8%, transportation 9.0% and services 2.0%. The breadth beyond fuel limited the Bank of Canada’s ability to treat the move solely as an external energy spike.

UK duration responded favorably to a political transition whose fiscal consequences remained unsettled. After Prime Minister Keir Starmer resigned, the 10-year gilt yield fell four basis points to 4.80%, while sterling edged higher against both the dollar and euro. The market response suggested no immediate assumption of unfunded spending. Reassurances that existing constraints on day-to-day spending and debt would remain in place helped, though any future change in the measurement horizon or bond-financed investment would reopen the supply question.

Japan presented the more persistent buyer-base challenge. Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi accepted the Bank of Japan’s latest increase even though she has long favored accommodation, and Deputy Governor Ryozo Himino warned that delaying adjustment could allow underlying inflation to exceed target. Nationwide consumer inflation held at 1.4% in May partly because of energy subsidies, while the BOJ judged high conflict-related energy prices to be an upside risk. Political acceptance gives the central bank more room to normalize when imported costs broaden.

Domestic demand for long JGBs was already changing. Insurers sold a net ¥201.2 billion of government bonds with more than 10 years to maturity in May as yields reached multi-decade highs, although pension-fund proxies made their largest purchases in almost two years. The 30-year yield near 3.9% remained below the 4.5% level one strategist associated with greater impairment risk and further insurer sales. This split matters for Treasuries because a higher domestic hurdle can reduce Japanese demand for hedged foreign duration even when pension buyers stabilize the local curve.

China supplied no new easing impulse through the June loan-prime-rate fixing. The one-year benchmark remained 3.00% and the over-five-year rate 3.50%, preserving existing bank-loan and mortgage references. The hold contrasted with rising policy rates elsewhere, but it also showed that weak domestic demand was not being met with a fresh headline-rate reduction. China’s low sovereign yields continue to offer an alternative duration pool, while the unchanged mortgage benchmark limits the near-term credit impulse.

Global transmission therefore ran through relative real yields, energy sensitivity and issuer funding choices. ECB restraint remained softer at the margin, Canada’s inflation breadth tightened its constraint, Japan’s policy and buyer base raised the domestic hurdle, and China held its stance. Those differences helped contain a common long-end selloff abroad, yet they did not supply enough global easing to offset the rise in US real yields.

## EM Sovereigns, Dollar Liquidity, And Debt-Management Risk

Emerging-market dollar debt faced a stronger benchmark and currency backdrop. EMB fell 0.3412%, while the US Dollar Index rose and a broad developing-currency gauge declined 0.25%. The Polish zloty, Philippine peso and Hungarian forint led losses. Progress in US-Iran talks supported emerging equities and cheaper oil, but expectations of additional Fed action dominated the currency channel, tightening conditions for external borrowers even without a generalized dollar-funding seizure.

Brazil illustrated how policy credibility can overwhelm near-term energy relief. The Treasury canceled a planned NTN-B inflation-linked bond auction after the central bank cut rates by 25 basis points while warning that growth and inflation had accelerated. Yields on CPI-linked bonds due May 2029 had risen more than 80 basis points during the month to 8.81%. Removing supply reduced immediate pressure, but it did not repair the conflict between easing policy, fiscal stimulus and above-target inflation.

The central bank also sold spot dollars while unwinding part of its FX-swap position. That paired operation was designed to prevent local dollar interest rates from rising further and to ease pressure on the currency carry trade. Swap futures declined and the real firmed slightly, outperforming most peers. The intervention addressed market liquidity and the Treasury action addressed near-term bond supply; neither resolved the underlying fiscal and inflation concerns that had weakened local duration.

Mexico used liability management to shift its maturity profile while rating pressure intensified. The sovereign marketed new 2037 bonds and a reopening of 2056 notes, with proceeds intended to repurchase dollar debt due in 2027 and 2028 and euro bonds due in 2029. S&P had moved the outlook to negative, and Moody’s had lowered the rating to the bottom investment-grade tier, citing fiscal weakness, rising debt and continued support for Pemex. The exchange can reduce near maturities, but execution terms determine the cost of extending duration.

Mexico’s scale makes that trade-off material. It sold $41 billion of debt in the prior year, much of it connected to Pemex support, then issued $9 billion in dollars and €9.5 billion in January. Large market access separates Mexico from sovereigns that cannot refinance conventionally, though repeated issuance also exposes the curve to concessions and rating-sensitive demand. The sovereign remains distinct from Pemex as an obligor even when support for the state oil company constrains the public balance sheet.

Argentina pursued lower-cost, partially guaranteed funding outside the international bond market. A decree authorized up to $5 billion of dollar borrowing from recognized financial institutions with partial backing from multilateral lenders. The immediate need is significant: almost $4.5 billion of bond payments falls due next month, and annual foreign-currency debt service is expected to exceed $20 billion next year. Multilateral guarantees could reduce the Treasury’s financing cost, but the final amount, tenor and guarantee structure will determine how much refinancing risk is actually displaced.

The EM pattern was debt-management dispersion under a higher US real-rate floor. Brazil used supply cancellation and FX liquidity to damp a credibility-driven local selloff; Mexico relied on deep market access to extend near maturities; Argentina sought credit enhancement because direct bond-market costs remained too high. Cheaper oil supported importers and reduced one source of inflation, yet the stronger dollar and Fed repricing continued to penalize sovereigns whose fiscal or policy framework left little margin for error.

## Cross asset

Equities showed concentrated stress rather than a uniform flight from risk. The S&P 500 fell 0.3705% and the Nasdaq 100 declined 0.1944%, while the semiconductor index gained 2.0426%. The VIX rose 5.3659% to 17.28. Technology hardware leadership remained intact, but the broader market had to absorb higher real discount rates and a sharp repricing in one of the largest AI-linked issuers.

SpaceX fell 16% to $154.60, extending its three-session loss to 23% and erasing more than $600 billion of market value. The shares still stood about 15% above the $135 IPO price, and only 4.2% of the company’s stock had been available to trade on the first day. Low float amplified the move, yet the coincidence of the decline with plans for a major debut bond sale linked equity volatility directly to concerns about capital intensity and future leverage.

Energy and inflation markets moved together. WTI fell 2.3238% to $74.82 and Brent lost 2.4421% to $77.90 as negotiators reported major progress toward a final US-Iran agreement. The declines extended to 7.3437% and 6.3364% over one week, respectively. Lower crude reinforced the fall in Treasury breakevens, but the simultaneous rise in nominal and real yields showed that energy relief could not override Fed policy repricing.

The dollar and gold completed the higher-real-rate configuration. The Dollar Index rose 0.1686% to 101.02, tightening the external backdrop for emerging-market borrowers. Gold fell 0.999% to $4,181.8999 as geopolitical demand eased and the opportunity cost of holding a non-yielding asset increased. Mixed equities, lower oil and gold, a firmer dollar and higher real yields described a policy-driven discount-rate adjustment with pockets of AI optimism, not a single-direction risk liquidation.

## Forward Market Significance

June flash purchasing-manager surveys on June 23 and the June 25 US release block provide the next test of whether Monday's selloff belongs in real yields or inflation compensation. The US block combines PCE inflation, personal income and spending, durable-goods orders, final first-quarter GDP and initial claims. Persistent services or core-price breadth alongside firm activity would validate Goolsbee's concern and the higher real-rate estimates, keeping the expected Fed path restrictive and pressure concentrated from two through 10-year real yields. Softer underlying inflation with slower demand, especially if breakevens stay contained and the curve rallies without sharp steepening, would challenge the equilibrium-rate narrative and restore the duration-relief interpretation that followed the FOMC.

Treasury absorption runs from the announced $69 billion two-year note on June 23 through the $70 billion five-year on June 24 and $44 billion seven-year on June 25, with bills, a floating-rate reopening and Treasury buybacks surrounding the coupon sequence. SpaceX and Sony marketing create a simultaneous high-grade test before either deal establishes completed demand. Broad direct and indirect Treasury participation, contained dealer awards, orderly cash yields, modest new-issue concessions and diversified corporate orderbooks would confirm that higher real rates are being transmitted through clearing prices while balance-sheet capacity remains available. Weak coupon demand, heavy dealer absorption or materially more expensive corporate execution would challenge Monday's orderly-funding conclusion, reopen a supply premium in the belly and high-grade duration, and make the concentration of June's issuance a larger constraint on subsequent borrowers.

The international energy and dollar channel is tested by Australia's May CPI on June 24, the BOJ Summary of Opinions and Bank of Canada deliberations that day, the EIA petroleum report, and the ECB bulletin on June 25. Slower price breadth, evidence of anchored wages, stable currencies and further crude normalization would confirm that diplomacy is containing second-round inflation, reduce foreign front-end competition with Treasuries and support the decline in US breakevens. Persistent core pressure, renewed oil tightness or hawkish policy debate would challenge that reading by lifting the global real-rate floor. Brazil's local dollar rates and inflation-linked yields, Mexico's liability-management pricing and Argentina's guaranteed funding terms provide the associated sovereign triggers: orderly execution would leave refinancing dispersion contained, whereas deterioration would reopen EM debt-management risk even if crude remains below its recent highs.

section_summaries.market_read: The selloff reflected a higher real-rate floor, not disorderly inflation or funding stress.
section_summaries.next_check: Incoming inflation breadth must show whether restrictive real-rate pricing remains justified.
section_summaries.summary: Lower oil reduced inflation compensation, but a firmer Fed reaction function lifted real yields and cheapened Treasuries across the curve. Active bill and corporate markets prevented a funding break, leaving the close as an orderly discount-rate reset complicated by a growing pipeline of technology-related borrowing.

section_summaries.market_read: Stable slopes keep the move centered on policy and real rates rather than a new term premium.
section_summaries.next_check: Services inflation and activity data will test whether the real-yield rise can persist.
section_summaries.section_title: UST Rates, Fed Pricing, Real Yields, And Curve Structure
section_summaries.summary: Yields rose almost in parallel, with modest five-year underperformance and no renewed long-end steepening. Falling breakevens, rising real yields, reduced Fed guidance and solid bill-auction demand together marked a restrictive policy repricing without evidence of immediate cash stress.

section_summaries.market_read: Credit access remains open, but duration-heavy supply is becoming a larger valuation constraint.
section_summaries.next_check: SpaceX and Sony execution will reveal whether demand extends beyond recent marquee deals.
section_summaries.summary: Investment-grade losses exceeded high-yield declines because duration, rather than broad credit flight, drove returns. Heavy technology issuance, SpaceX's prospective debut, offshore borrowing and convertible structures showed capital remained available, although concentration and future AI cash needs could make absorption more selective.

section_summaries.market_read: Global divergence softened common duration pressure without delivering a broad easing impulse.
section_summaries.next_check: Inflation breadth, wage persistence and Japanese buyer behavior remain the key external checks.
section_summaries.section_title: Global Duration, Central Banks, And Fiscal Transmission
section_summaries.summary: Global rates diverged as the ECB gained conditional patience, Canadian inflation broadened, Japan preserved its normalization bias and China held lending benchmarks. The split contained a synchronized overseas selloff, but higher foreign policy hurdles and shifting Japanese demand still competed with Treasury duration.

section_summaries.market_read: EM risk remains differentiated by policy credibility and refinancing capacity under a firm dollar.
section_summaries.next_check: Brazilian local rates and Mexico and Argentina funding terms will test whether dispersion stays contained.
section_summaries.section_title: EM Sovereigns, Dollar Liquidity, And Debt-Management Risk
section_summaries.summary: A stronger dollar and higher US real-rate floor tightened conditions for emerging sovereigns without causing a generalized funding seizure. Brazil managed local supply and currency liquidity, Mexico extended maturities through deep market access, and Argentina pursued guaranteed borrowing to reduce refinancing costs.

section_summaries.market_read: Cross-assets confirm a real-rate repricing with selective risk stress, not broad liquidation.
section_summaries.next_check: Oil normalization and technology financing conditions will show whether the pattern can hold.
section_summaries.summary: Mixed equities, lower oil and gold, a firmer dollar and higher real yields formed a policy-driven discount-rate pattern rather than a uniform risk retreat. Semiconductor strength contrasted with broader weakness, while SpaceX's slide linked equity volatility to capital intensity and prospective leverage.

section_summaries.market_read: The outlook hinges on real-rate validation, supply absorption and global energy pass-through.
section_summaries.next_check: The June 23-25 data and issuance sequence is the immediate test of all three channels.
section_summaries.summary: Near-term US data, Treasury coupon auctions and major corporate deals will test whether higher real yields can coexist with orderly absorption. Global inflation, central-bank signals and sovereign funding outcomes will separately determine whether cheaper energy can contain foreign rate competition and EM refinancing strain.

methodology_note: Market levels are shown on an NY-close basis unless otherwise noted.

bullets: 10Y TIPS real yield 2.28% (+7 bp) | 10Y breakeven inflation 2.23% (-2 bp)
UST 2s10s curve 27 bp (0 bp) | UST 2s30s curve 71 bp (0 bp)
overall_summary.headline: Treasuries sold off as Fed repricing lifted real yields despite cheaper oil
overall_summary.session_narrative: Treasuries ended the NY session lower across maturities, with the belly slightly lagging and real yields rising faster than nominal yields. A more data-responsive Fed and persistent services inflation kept the policy path firm, while progress in US-Iran talks lowered oil and breakevens. Strong bill demand and active corporate financing limited the move to an orderly repricing, though concentrated technology borrowing and global policy divergence kept competition for capital elevated.
overall_summary.summary: The Treasury curve cheapened broadly while weekly flattening held, leaving the close defined by higher real discount rates rather than renewed inflation compensation. Credit access stayed open, but a heavy issuance pipeline and a firmer dollar preserved funding pressure.

watch_events.market_implication: Pressure would remain concentrated in two- through 10-year real yields; softer data would revive duration relief.
watch_events.market_trigger: Persistent core and services pressure alongside firm activity would validate higher real yields.
watch_events.title: US growth and inflation data
watch_events.watch_line: Flash PMIs and the June 25 release block test inflation breadth against demand.

watch_events.market_implication: Poor execution would reopen a belly and high-grade supply premium and constrain later borrowers.
watch_events.market_trigger: Broad participation and modest concessions would confirm orderly absorption; weak demand would not.
watch_events.title: Treasury and corporate supply absorption
watch_events.watch_line: Coupon auctions and SpaceX and Sony marketing will test balance-sheet capacity.

watch_events.market_implication: Renewed oil tightness or hawkish debate would raise the global real-rate floor and EM refinancing risk.
watch_events.market_trigger: Slower price breadth, anchored wages, stable currencies and softer crude would confirm containment.
watch_events.title: Global energy and dollar transmission
watch_events.watch_line: Inflation releases, policy records and oil data will test whether second-round effects stay contained.
